events.change_summary: Full scope — Requirements (17), Acceptance Criteria (12), 3 implementation phases with detailed files and verification

events.rationale: CLI-only feature across 7 files — no design stage needed (follows existing Rich table patterns) and no architecture stage needed (all three additions build directly on established patterns: cymru_bulk_lookup_rich() exists, Atlas scheduling pipeline exists, globe.py exists). Tier is full due to new subcommand, cross-cutting enrichment changes, and README docs spanning the entire Atlas feature surface.

events.decision: Reuse cymru_bulk_lookup_rich() for org name enrichment rather than introducing a new lookup service
events.rationale: cymru_bulk_lookup_rich() already exists in asn.py (lines 77–113), returns {ip: {asn, prefix, name}}, and is already used in display.py. It performs a batch whois query with no new runtime dependency. Adding a separate ASN-to-name service (e.g., PeeringDB org lookup by ASN) would add latency, a new external dependency, and cache complexity. The Cymru IP-based lookup is consistent with how ASNs are already resolved throughout the codebase.

events.decision: Atlas anchor fallback submits anchor IDs through the existing measurement scheduling pipeline unchanged
events.rationale: The RIPE Atlas scheduling/polling/parsing flow in atlas.py and cli.py accepts a list of probe IDs. Anchor nodes have numeric IDs in the same ID space, so no parallel code path is needed. The only additions are: (1) find_anchors_in_asn() to discover anchor IDs from the /api/v2/anchors/ endpoint, and (2) a source flag to label results as anchor-sourced in the output. This keeps the fallback path thin and the existing scheduling code untouched.

events.summary: Full scope written as revision 2 — 19 requirements across 4 categories, 12 acceptance criteria, 3 phases (Medium / Small / Large). Phase 1 wires cymru_bulk_lookup_rich() into hubs and display for org names. Phase 2 adds find_anchors_in_asn() fallback with [Atlas anchor] labeling. Phase 3 adds the atlas-profile subcommand with choropleth globe and README docs. Architecture skipped — all patterns are established. Workflow: backend-feature, full tier, scope → implementation → review → release.

workflow.rationale: CLI-only feature across 7 files — no design stage needed (follows existing Rich table patterns) and no architecture stage needed (all three additions build directly on established patterns: cymru_bulk_lookup_rich() exists, Atlas scheduling pipeline exists, globe.py exists). Tier is full due to new subcommand, cross-cutting enrichment changes, and README docs spanning the entire Atlas feature surface.
